Thought-provoking Opinions, Expert Analysis, In-depth Insights and other Member Only BenefitsAfter witnessing a mostly dry winter season, Himachal Pradesh is set to experience scorching heat in the upcoming summer season as the total number of heat wave days are expected to be high across the state as compared to normal.
As per the State’s Meteorological Department, there is moderate probability (35-65 per cent) that the state will experience a greater number of heat wave days as compared to the long-term average across the state.
Also, maximum and minimum temperatures are very likely to be above normal in most parts of the state till June 2026. The department has forecasted that there is moderate to high probability (45-75 per cent) that the minimum temperatures across the state will very likely be above normal between April to June.
Similarly, maximum temperatures in high and mid hills of the state will also be above normal, with 45 per cent to 65 per cent probability. Also, there is a 45 per cent to 65 per cent probability of maximum temperatures being normal in low hills and plains of the state in the same period of time.
The department has revealed that there is low to moderate probability (33.5-55 per cent) that many parts of the state will get above normal rainfall during April and there is low to moderate probability (33.5-55 per cent) that few parts of Lahaul and Spiti and Kinnaur districts will experience normal to below normal rainfall.
Also, there is 55-75 per cent probability of the state witnessing above normal minimum temperatures in most parts of the state in April. Additionally, there is a 45 to 75 per cent probability that maximum temperatures will be above normal in most parts of the state in April.
Meanwhile, the state in March witnessed 12 per cent rainfall deficit in March as the state received 99.6 mm rain as compared to 113.4 mm, which is considered normal for March. With Chamba receiving 119.7 mm rain as compared to 177.4 mm normal, it emerged as the driest district of the state as it recorded 33 per cent rainfall deficit. Similarly, the highest rain in the state was recorded in Solan district which received 99.6 mm as compared to 61.9 mm normal rain, thus recording 61 per cent surplus rain.
The department has also forecasted heavy snowfall and rain for the state till April 7, with the state witnessing intensified showers on April 3 and 4. The department has also issued an orange weather warning for Shimla, Kullu, Kangra, Mandi and Chamba districts for April 3 and 4. Additionally, it has also issued yellow weather warnings for various districts of the state from April 3 to 5.
